content: Which one of the following is the definition of ultimate tensile strength (UTS) obtained from a stress-strain test on a metal specimen?

options.content: Stress value where the stress-strain curve transitions from elastic to plastic behavior
The maximum load attained divided by the original cross-sectional area
The maximum load attained divided by the corresponding instantaneous cross-sectional area

explanation:
The ultimate tensile strength (UTS) obtained from a stress-strain test on a metal specimen is defined as the maximum load attained divided by the original cross-sectional area.

$$\sigma_{ult} = \frac{P_{max}}{A_0}$$

Where :

$\sigma_{ult}$ = Ultimate tensile strength

$P_{max}$ = Maximum load obtained in a tensile test

$A_0$ = Original cross-sectional area before the load is applied

Hence, the correct option is (B).

content: A free bar of length 1 m is uniformly heated from $${0^ \circ }C$$  to a temperature of $${t^ \circ }C$$. $$\alpha $$ is the coefficient if linear expansion and $$E$$ the modulus of elasticity. The stress in the bar is
A large uniform plate containing a rivet-hole is subjected to uniform uniaxial tension of 95 MPa. The maximum stress in the plate is

Cylindrical bars P and Q have identical lengths and radii, but are composed of different linear elastic materials. The Young’s modulus and coefficient of thermal expansion of Q are twice the corresponding values of P. Assume the bars to be perfectly bonded at the interface, and their weights to be negligible.

The bars are held between rigid supports as shown in the figure and the temperature is raised by Δ𝑇. Assume that the stress in each bar is homogeneous and uniaxial. Denote the magnitudes of stress in P and Q by σ1 and σ2, respectively.

Which of the statement(s) given is/are CORRECT?

options.content: The interface between P and Q moves to the left after heating
The interface between P and Q moves to the right after heating
σ1 < σ2
σ1 = σ2

explanation:
According to question,

Where, $E=$ Modulus of elasticity, $\alpha=$ Coefficient of thermal expansion .

As we know form question bar is fixed in both sides

Apply $\Sigma F_x=0$,

Stress in bar $(P)=\frac{R}{A}=\sigma_1$ ........(i)

Stress in bar $(Q)=\frac{R}{A}=\sigma_2$ ........(ii)

From equations (i) and (ii),

$$ \sigma_1=\sigma_2 $$

So, option (D) will correct Apply compatibility equation

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Delta l_{\text {total }}=0 \\\\ & l \alpha \Delta T-\frac{\sigma_1 l}{E}+2 l \alpha \Delta T-\frac{\sigma_1 l}{2 E}=0 \\\\ & \frac{3 \sigma_1 l}{2 E}=3 l \alpha \Delta T \\\\ & \sigma_1=2 E \alpha \Delta T \end{aligned} $$

Change in length in bar $P=l \alpha \Delta-\frac{\sigma_{\mathrm{l}} l}{E}=l \alpha \Delta T-2 l \alpha \Delta T=-l \alpha \Delta T$

Minus sign shows that bar $P$ compress so this interface will moves towards left.

Hence the correct option are (A) and (D).

content:
Ignoring the small elastic region, the true stress (𝜎) – true strain (𝜀) variation of a material beyond yielding follows the equation 𝜎 = 400𝜀0.3 MPa. The engineering ultimate tensile strength value of this material is ________ MPa.

(Rounded off to one decimal place)
explanation:
Engineering stress-strain is lower than true stress-strain because engineering analysis considers only the initial area throughout the process, while the true stress-strain curve accounts for the instantaneous area.

Mathematically,

$ \rm{Engineering~Stress}\;(σ_o) = \frac{\text{Applied Force}}{\text{Initial Area}} $

$ \rm{True~Stress}\;(σ_t) = \frac{\text{Applied Force}}{\text{Instantaneous Area}} $

The relationship between true stress (σt) and engineering stress (σo) is given by :

$ σ_t = σ_o (1 + ε_o) $

$ ε_t = \ln (1 + ε_o) $

where σo is the engineering stress, σt is the true stress, εo is the engineering strain, and εt is the true strain.

Given :

$ σ_T = 400ε_T^{0.3} $

At the ultimate tensile strength,

n = εT = 0.3

Therefore, the true stress at ultimate tensile strength is :

$ σ_T = 400 \times 0.3^{0.3} = 278.74 \text{ MPa} $

The true strain is given by :

$ ε_T = \ln(1 + ε) $

Thus,

$ 1 + ε = e^{0.3} = 1.35 $

The engineering ultimate tensile strength is :

$ σ = \frac{σ_T}{1 + ε} = \frac{278.74}{1.35} = 206.55 \text{ MPa} $
